---
title: "What Indian packaged food labels told us about fortification - and what they couldn't"
date: 2026-03-26
categories: [ifid, fortification, data-analysis, track-a]
author:
  - name: Lalitha A R
    affiliation: iSRL
    orcid: 0009-0001-7466-3531
    email: lalithaar.research@gmail.com
    corresponding: true
description: "92 label rows. 68 canonical agents. Four nutrient classes. What the label evidence showed, what it structurally could not show, and why that distinction matters for how IFID models fortification."
keywords:
  - food fortification India
  - FSSAI fortification agents
  - packaged food labels India
  - ingredient identity fortification
  - Indian food data
  - food labelling standards India
  - vitamins minerals packaged food
format:
  html:
    toc: true
license: "CC BY 4.0"
citation: true
open-graph:
  title: "What Indian packaged food labels told us about fortification - and what they couldn't"
  description: "When we read 92 label rows for fortification agents, we found 68 distinct canonical substances - and a structural gap in how Indian labels declare fortification that no amount of curation can fix."
twitter-card:
  title: "What Indian packaged food labels told us about fortification - and what they couldn't"
  description: "92 label rows. 68 canonical agents. And a finding the data couldn't produce on its own: why 'fortified' on an Indian label can mean almost anything."
  card-style: summary
status: draft
---

## What "fortified" means on an Indian label - and why it isn't consistent

Walk down the functional foods aisle of any Indian supermarket and you will
see the word "fortified" on packaging for everything from atta to whey protein
to children's biscuits. The word implies addition - something has been put into
this food that wasn't there before. But the regulatory meaning of "fortified"
varies sharply depending on the product category.

For edible oil and wheat flour, fortification with specific vitamins and
minerals is a government mandate under FSSAI's Food Fortification programme -
a public health intervention targeting micronutrient deficiencies across the
population. For sports nutrition products, "fortified" often means the brand
chose to add amino acids or creatine because their target customer wants them.
Both are legal. Both appear on labels. Neither declaration tells you which
kind of fortification you are looking at.

This inconsistency is not a labelling failure. It is a feature of how the word
is used - and it is the first thing the label data made visible.

---

## What this analysis set out to answer

IFID is building a reference layer for ingredients in Indian packaged food -
a shared identity record for each substance that appears on labels, stable
enough that researchers, practitioners, regulators, and journalists can work
from the same foundation. For fortification to be part of that reference layer,
several things need to be known: which agents appear on Indian labels, how they
cluster by type, where the evidence is strong and where it is thin, and - most
importantly - what the current label format can and cannot express about the
relationship between a fortifying agent and the food it was added to.

This analysis reads 92 label rows to answer those questions. It closes Track A
of a two-track analysis. Track B, which addresses the regulatory picture -
what FSSAI mandates, for which categories, in which forms - is a separate body
of work initiated as a GitHub issue linked at the end of this document.

---

## The label evidence: what we found

### Scale and source

92 source label rows were processed - 72 from the working CSV and 20 from a
pending enrichment file - producing 68 canonical fortification agent entries.
The reference list closed on 2026-03-19.

`Source: fortification-taxonomy/fortification-taxonomy_clean_2026-03-19.json`

### What didn't make it in - and why

10 source rows were excluded from the 68 canonical entries.

**4 structural exclusions:**

Three rows were compound wheat flour declarations - label text of the form
"fortified wheat flour thiamin" or "fortified wheat flour niacin." These bundle
the carrier ingredient and the added nutrient into a single unstructured string.
The nutrients themselves (thiamin, niacin) are present in the reference list as
standalone entries. The compound form is a label format problem, not a missing
substance - and what it reveals about current labelling practice matters enough
to have its own section below.

One row was L-HPC (low-substituted hydroxypropyl cellulose) - a pharmaceutical
tablet binder that appeared because some product labels mix food and pharmaceutical
conventions. L-HPC is not a food fortification agent under any regulatory
definition. It was removed.

**6 reclassified from fortification to health supplement:**

L-carnitine, creatine monohydrate micronised, whey peptides, glutamine peptides,
and calcium HMB (β-hydroxy-β-methylbutyrate monohydrate). These are functional
and sports nutrition ingredients. They appeared on labels that describe them as
fortification agents, but they fall under FSSAI's health supplement and
nutraceutical regulations, not food fortification standards.

This reclassification makes the inconsistency visible in the data. The word
"fortified" on a label covers everything from a government-mandated vitamin A
addition in refined oil to an optional creatine inclusion in a gym supplement.
The reference list draws that line. The labels do not.[^1]

[^1]: The 6 reclassified rows now sit in a stub folder at `health_supplement/`.
That category will need its own analysis pass.

### Agents by nutrient class

| Nutrient class | Canonical entries | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Mineral Nutrients | 33 | Includes 5 group declarations (Ferrous Salt, Calcium Salt, Salts of Magnesium, Iodate forms, Chloride) |
| Water-Soluble Vitamins | 16 | B1, B2, B3, B6, B9, B12, Biotin, Pantothenic Acid, Choline; plus Vitamin B Complex (group) |
| Amino Acids | 13 | Arginine, Glutamine, Leucine, Lysine, L-Lysine HCl, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Taurine, Threonine, Valine, plus Amino Acids (group) |
| Fat-Soluble Vitamins | 6 | Vitamin A, Retinyl Acetate, Retinyl Palmitate, Vitamin D, Ergocalciferol (D2), Cholecalciferol (D3) |
| **Total** | **68** | 7 of the 68 are group declarations |

Minerals account for 33 of 68 entries - just under half. This reflects the
form specificity of mineral fortification: zinc appears as zinc sulphate, zinc
sulphate monohydrate, and zinc gluconate as distinct entries because the form
matters for bioavailability and regulatory matching. Magnesium appears in 9
distinct forms.[^2]

[^2]: Magnesium forms in the reference list: aspartate, bisglycinate, carbonate,
gluconate, hydroxide, oxide, phosphate dibasic, sulphate, and threonate - plus
the group declaration "Salts of Magnesium."

7 of the 68 entries are group declarations - labels that name a class rather
than a specific substance (Vitamin B Complex, Amino Acids, Electrolytes,
Ferrous Salt, Calcium Salt, Salts of Magnesium, Chloride). These are included
because they appear on labels and need to be matched, but they cannot be tracked
to a single substance. They are present in the reference list with a flag.

### Where the agents come from

Source type is recorded for most of the 68 entries:

| Source type | Entry count |
|---|---|
| Synthetic | 36 |
| Mineral salt | 31 |
| Fermentation (corn) | 3 |
| Fermentation (sugarcane) | 3 |
| Fish liver oil | 4 |
| Lanolin | 2 |
| Palm | 1 |
| Yeast | 1 |

Source type carries practical significance: it determines allergen tagging,
halal and vegan flags, and origin declarations in some regulatory contexts.
Fish liver oil is the source for some vitamin A and D entries. Lanolin - derived
from sheep wool - is the source for cholecalciferol (D3). These distinctions
are invisible on a label that simply declares "Vitamin D3."

### Data confidence

Zone distribution across the 72 working CSV rows before pending enrichment:

- Zone 2 (higher confidence): 48 rows (66.7%)
- Zone 1 (lower confidence, flagged for review): 24 rows (33.3%)

OCR and transcription artefacts resolved during curation: `sodium img` →
Sodium Iodate; `phenylalaline` → Phenylalanine; `l-camitine` → L-Carnitine
(then reclassified to health supplement).

---

## Where the label evidence is strong and where it is thin

Most of what these labels captured comes from health supplement and sports
nutrition products - not from conventional staple food fortification. Amino
acids (arginine, leucine, taurine) do not appear in atta. Magnesium threonate
is not in milk. The 68-entry reference list is weighted toward the supplement
sector because supplement labels are more explicit about what has been added.

Conventional fortification in staple foods - oils with vitamins A and D, flours
with B vitamins and iron - tends to appear in compound ingredient declarations
("fortified wheat flour thiamin") or not as a separate field at all. This is
not a gap in the reference list. The agents are present. But the label evidence
that built the list came mostly from supplement products, not from the staple
food categories where government-mandated fortification is most consequential
for public health.

The gap matters because the reference list was built from supplement-weighted
evidence for a domain - staple food fortification - where that evidence is thin.
The agents are right. The coverage question is whether they are complete for
categories the label sample barely touched.

---

## The deeper problem: labels name agents, not relationships

The reference list contains 68 agents. What it does not contain - and what
the label format structurally cannot provide - is the relationship between an
agent and the ingredient it was added to.

This gap has two manifestations in the data.

The first is the three excluded compound declarations. "Fortified wheat flour
thiamin" puts the carrier (wheat flour) and the agent (thiamin) into a single
string with no delimiter. The label format provides no mechanism to express
this as a relationship. Both facts are in the string; neither can be extracted
cleanly without interpreting the whole phrase as a unit. The three rows were
excluded not because the information wasn't there, but because the format
bundled it inseparably.

The second is the mirror problem across the other 89 rows. They name agents
cleanly - electrolytic iron, riboflavin, retinyl palmitate - but carry no
carrier attribution. A product that declares "fortified with vitamins A and D"
while containing edible oil, milk solids, and a premix blend does not indicate
which ingredient was fortified. All three could be carriers. The label is not
incomplete in any regulatory sense - it describes what the product contains.
But it is not a schema.

The 68-entry reference list reflects what the labels made available: a catalogue
of agents, without carrier-agent pairs. That is not a curation failure. It is
the limit of what the current label format expresses.

IFID's design models fortification differently. An ingredient entry - edible
oil, wheat flour - carries a fortification flag. When the flag is set, the
agent field records what was added to that ingredient. The carrier is the entry
itself. The relationship is structural, not textual.

Whether the regulatory framework and actual label practice support building
carrier-agent pairs from current data - or whether doing so requires inference,
primary FSSAI documents, or a new kind of labelling ask - is the central
question Track B is tasked with answering.

---

## Unverified FSSAI notes from enrichment

During enrichment, a model pass pulled FSSAI mandatory fortification references
for 5 food categories. These are unverified - they came from an enrichment
model run, not from primary FSSAI documents, and are flagged as such in the
reference list. Track B will verify.

| Food category | Agents noted (unverified) |
|---|---|
| Edible oil | Vitamins A, D |
| Wheat flour | B1, B2, B3, B9, B12, Iron, Zinc |
| Rice | B1, B2, B3, B12, Iron, Zinc, Folic Acid |
| Milk | Vitamins A, D, B12, Calcium, Zinc |
| Salt | Iodine (and sometimes Iron, Zinc) |

All agents listed above are present in the 68-entry reference list. The agents
exist. What the label data did not show is whether these specific carrier
categories - oil, flour, rice, milk, salt - were the ones carrying those agents
on the labels processed. The agents were visible; the carriers were not.

---

## What this analysis answered, and what it did not

**Answered:**

- What fortification agents appear on Indian packaged food labels, by name and
  canonical form
- How 68 distinct canonical entries cluster by nutrient class and source type
- Why 10 source rows were excluded, and what those exclusions reveal about
  label convention
- Where the label evidence is strong (supplement sector) and where it is thin
  (conventional staple fortification)
- Why current label format produces agent data but not carrier-agent pairs -
  and why that gap is structural, not a curation problem

**Not answered by this analysis:**

- Which ingredient categories are legally required to carry fortification under
  FSSAI, and what that means for how IFID models the relationship
- Whether the 68-entry reference list is complete for the Indian F&B FMCG
  context, particularly for categories the label sample barely touched
- Whether regulatory and label practice support building carrier-agent pairs
  from current data, or whether a different approach is needed

These questions are the scope of Track B.
See: [GitHub Issue #- - Track B: Fortification regulatory scope and FMCG coverage](#)

---

## Data files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `fortification_agent/fortification_agent_working.csv` | Source rows, zone distribution |
| `fortification_agent/pending_enrichment.csv` | 20 additional rows |
| `fortification_agent/fortification-taxonomy/fortification-taxonomy_clean_2026-03-19.json` | 68 canonical entries, nutrient class counts, source types |
| `fortification_agent/fortification-taxonomy/taxonomy_log.md` | Source accounting |
| `fortification_agent/cp/README_fortification.md` | Exclusion decisions, session log |

---

## Note on AI authorship

This document was drafted by Claude (Anthropic, Sonnet 4.6) in a Claude Code
session on 2026-03-26, from a structured plan written by Lalitha A R after
the fortification curation was complete.

Claude verified all counts against the source files before drafting any claim.
All prose, tables, footnotes, and section structure are Claude's. iSRL tone
constraints applied throughout. The carrier-agent declaration gap observation
is Claude's interpretation of the structural exclusion data, reviewed and
accepted by Lalitha A R.

No novel analysis was run. No regulatory claims were made without the
unverified flag. No gaps were filled with inference - where the data did not
answer a question, the document says so.
